title: The Deal Arrives: US-Iran Agreement Opens Hormuz as Markets Rally
subhead: After nearly four months of war that reshaped global energy flows, the US and Iran signed a memorandum of understanding to end hostilities and reopen the Strait of Hormuz. The deal brings immediate relief to markets but defers the hardest questions — nuclear limits, sanctions relief, and Lebanon's fate — to 60 days of negotiations that begin this week.

sections.heading: US-Iran Deal Opens Hormuz, Defers Nuclear Questions
sections.body:
President Donald Trump announced on Sunday that a memorandum of understanding with Iran is 'now complete,' marking the most significant diplomatic breakthrough in a conflict that began with joint US-Israeli strikes on February 28. The agreement, brokered primarily by Pakistan with Qatari support, is scheduled for formal signing on Friday in Switzerland.

The core terms restore prewar status quo: Iran reopens the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20% of global oil supplies flow, and the US lifts its blockade of Iranian ports. Trump declared 'Ships of the World, start your engines. Let the oil flow!' — though shippers remain cautious about mine clearance operations that could take weeks.

Critical issues remain unresolved. The 60-day negotiation period will address Iran's nuclear program, including the fate of highly enriched uranium believed buried under damaged nuclear sites, and sanctions relief tied to frozen Iranian assets. Vice President JD Vance, described by Senator Lindsey Graham as 'the architect of the deal,' said he expects the full text to be released this week. The agreement reportedly releases half of Iran's frozen funds before final talks, according to CBS News.

Iran views the deal as a victory, having survived the most serious attempt by the US and Israel to topple the Islamic Republic. Tehran demonstrated its ability to shut down the Strait of Hormuz and strike Gulf allies, a capability that may shape future deterrence calculations.
sections.whyItMatters: The deal ends a conflict that killed thousands, drove up global inflation, and tested Gulf alliances. But the framework leaves Iran's nuclear status unclear and its regional alliances intact — raising questions about whether this is a lasting peace or an interlude before the next crisis.

sections.heading: Lebanon Gap: Israel Refuses Withdrawal as Hezbollah Pledges Resistance
sections.body:
Iran conditioned the agreement on ending fighting in Lebanon, where Israel and Hezbollah have been at war since March 2. But Israel's Defense Minister Israel Katz declared Monday that Israeli forces will remain 'indefinitely' in areas of southern Lebanon seized during the conflict — directly challenging the deal's framework.

According to Reuters, Israeli officials privately view the agreement as 'terrible for Israel,' with Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu largely sidelined from negotiations. Trump criticized an Israeli airstrike on Beirut that occurred hours before the deal announcement, saying it 'should not have happened, particularly on a special day when we are so close to a peace deal with Iran.'

Hezbollah welcomed the deal in a Monday statement, saying Iran secured Lebanon's inclusion in the agreement. The group said it remains committed to 'resisting Israel until full withdrawal is achieved.' The pace of Israeli strikes in Lebanon has decreased dramatically since the announcement, Lebanese security sources told Reuters, but the fundamental dispute over territory remains unresolved.

The Israel-Hezbollah front — not party to the US-Iran agreement — could still unravel the broader framework. If fighting escalates, Iran faces pressure to respond, potentially dragging negotiations back into crisis.
sections.whyItMatters: The deal's weakest link is Lebanon. Israel's refusal to withdraw and Hezbollah's commitment to continued resistance create a fault line that could collapse the agreement if violence resumes.

sections.heading: Markets Rally as Oil Prices Fall to Three-Month Low
sections.body:
Brent crude futures fell approximately 5% on Monday, reaching a three-month low, after Trump's announcement. European natural gas prices dropped sharply, and stock markets rallied globally. The dollar weakened as investors absorbed the de-escalation news.

Japanese shippers welcomed the agreement but are awaiting details on mine clearance before allowing vessels through the Strait, Reuters reports. The waterway remains dangerous until naval forces can certify safe passage. France and Britain announced plans to lead a multinational demining mission, with French President Emmanuel Macron saying assets including a carrier strike group already in the region could deploy within days.

The IMF published an assessment Monday noting the global economy 'has endured the war shock so far,' but warning that resilience masks significant differences between energy importers and exporters. Countries dependent on Gulf energy faced inflation spikes and growth pressure, while oil producers benefited from elevated prices.

The question now is whether oil prices stabilize at prewar levels — around $80 per barrel according to Yahoo Finance — or remain elevated due to damaged infrastructure and ongoing risk premiums.
sections.whyItMatters: The immediate market reaction signals relief, but the speed of returning oil flows depends on mine clearance and whether shippers regain confidence. Prolonged caution could keep prices above prewar baselines.

sections.heading: G7 Summit Opens With Iran Deal — and Tariff Tensions
sections.body:
Trump arrived at the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains, France, on Monday with wind at his back after the Iran announcement. But the gathering also exposed transatlantic friction that predates the conflict. Before departing, Trump told the New York Post he warned French President Emmanuel Macron that the US will 'have no choice' but to impose 100% tariffs on French wines unless Paris eliminates its digital tax on American tech companies — renewing a threat from his first administration.

Macron pushed back Monday, saying 'it is not for the United States to decide what European or French law should be.' The exchange sets up potential confrontation at a summit already complicated by the Iran deal's implementation and the Ukraine war.

The European leaders of the UK, France, Germany, and Italy issued a joint statement welcoming the Iran agreement as a 'moment of opportunity to restore regional stability and stabilize the global economy.' But according to AP News, Trump has pushed back on those same leaders for lacking support during the conflict, while they criticized his failure to consult allies before launching the war.

Macron invited Egypt, Qatar, and the UAE to Tuesday's Middle East session, and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy will attend for discussions on the Russia-Ukraine war. Zelenskyy and Trump held a phone call Sunday, with Zelenskyy saying Ukraine's eastern front has 'improved and strengthened.'
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran deal gives Trump diplomatic momentum, but underlying tensions with European allies over trade and burden-sharing remain. The G7's ability to present a united front on Iran and Ukraine will be tested.

sections.heading: Russia Strikes Kyiv's Sacred Lavra as Zelenskyy Appeals to G7
sections.body:
Hours before the G7 summit, Russia launched one of its largest attacks on Ukraine in months, firing over 600 drones and 70 missiles at Kyiv, Kharkiv, and Dnipro. The overnight barrage killed 11 people, including five rescue workers in Kharkiv, and wounded 53, according to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy.

The strike set fire to the Dormition Cathedral inside the Kyiv-Pechersk Lavra, an 11th-century UNESCO World Heritage site and one of Eastern Orthodox Christianity's most sacred landmarks. Metropolitan Epiphanius, head of the Orthodox Church of Ukraine, condemned the attack as a crime 'against humanity, against history, against Christianity.' Zelenskyy called it Russia's 'biggest crime yet against Christian culture.'

French Foreign Minister Jean-Noel Barrot compared the attack to 'a bombing of Notre Dame' in Paris. Ukraine's National Commission for UNESCO urged the international community to increase pressure on Moscow.

The timing — after separate phone calls between Trump, Zelenskyy, and Putin on Sunday — signals that Moscow remains unwilling to halt offensive operations despite diplomatic engagement. Zelenskyy will attend the G7 on Tuesday to push for more air defense support, particularly anti-ballistic missile capabilities.
sections.whyItMatters: The attack demonstrates that Russia continues large-scale strikes even as diplomatic channels remain open. The damage to a UNESCO World Heritage site may increase pressure on European allies to sustain support for Ukraine.

sections.heading: Myanmar's Junta Leader Visits Beijing as China Courts Pariah States
sections.body:
Myanmar's Min Aung Hlaing arrived in Beijing on Monday for a five-day visit at the invitation of Chinese President Xi Jinping, Reuters reports. The trip marks the first visit to China by the junta chief since he became the country's president — a title he assumed after a military coup that overthrew a civilian government.

China has maintained relations with Myanmar's junta despite international condemnation, balancing strategic interests against human rights concerns. Myanmar sits on China's southern flank and hosts infrastructure projects critical to Beijing's Belt and Road Initiative, including ports and pipelines that provide alternative routes to the Strait of Malacca.

The visit follows a pattern of China engaging with isolated regimes — from Iran to North Korea to now Myanmar's military leadership — as geopolitical competition with the US intensifies. The Australian newspaper noted that Beijing's welcome for Min Aung Hlaing shows 'geopolitics trumps principles' as China prioritizes strategic positioning over Western concerns.
sections.whyItMatters: China continues building relationships with states outside the Western order, offering alternatives to US-led frameworks. As Iran stabilizes, Beijing's engagement with pariah regimes persists.

bottomLine: The US-Iran deal announced Sunday marks the most consequential diplomatic development since the war began in February, reopening the Strait of Hormuz and restoring prewar energy flows. But the framework defers the hardest questions — nuclear limits, sanctions relief, and Israel's presence in Lebanon — to 60 days of negotiations that begin this week. Markets rallied on the news, but the deal's durability depends on whether Lebanon's unresolved conflict and Iran's nuclear status can be settled without reigniting broader hostilities. Meanwhile, Russia's attack on Kyiv's sacred Lavra and China's welcome for Myanmar's junta chief demonstrate that even with one war cooling, other fronts remain active.
